blocks.text: Magic
Glorantha's magic is introduced in this chapter, with the chapters following it describing the specific magical approaches in greater detail.

Gloranthans practise many different types of magic which anyone can learn, provided they are of the correct species, ethnic culture or members of a cult which teaches it. These arts are called Folk Magic, Spirit Magic, Rune Magic, Sorcery and Lunar Magic. The incomprehensible art of Mysticism is primarily reserved for the Draconic races.

As described in chapter XX, all magic is drawn from the Runes; the fundamental building blocks of Gloranthan reality. Certain cultures and species possess inherent bonds to certain Runes, called Rune Affinities, which often limit the cults, traditions and schools which they may join — and in turn restrict the particular spells or spirits which they can then master.

blocks.heading: Folk Magic
blocks.parenthetical: RQ2 Battle Magic, RQ3 Spirit Magic, RQ6 Folk Magic
blocks.text: Folk Magic is the simplest form of magic in Glorantha, commonly available to all cultures. It is taught from an early age, but in terms of power offers only limited effect. Particular spells reflect the everyday necessities of a community, profession or cult which provides them. This means that Folk Magic taught to a barbarian goat herd will often differ to that taught to a civilized sailor. Due to its petty nature, Folk Magic spells are not overtly supernatural, instead tending to augment natural processes, the surrounding environment or the capabilities of the caster.

blocks.heading: Rune Magic
blocks.parenthetical: RQ2 Rune Magic, RQ3 Divine Magic, RQ6 Theism
blocks.text: Rune Magic is drawn from the gods, demi-gods and heroes of Glorantha, provided the worshipper performs the correct rituals to invoke the deity and perform regular sacrifices to honour or propitiate them. In effect, the god intercedes directly on behalf of the worshipper; either locally manifesting their power through a Rune they are linked with, or incarnating directly within the body of the worshipper to perform an epic feat. Due to the ubiquity of religious cults across the world, Rune Magic is the most frequently encountered higher magic in Glorantha.

blocks.heading: Sorcery
blocks.text: A less well regarded form of magic by many, sorcery uses immutable laws to exploit the Runes directly, without needing to call upon the gods or bargain with spirits. Such spells are impersonal and formulaic, normally controlling only the material aspects of the world. Yet sorcery is feared by those practicing Spirit and Rune Magic, since sorcerers are capable of briefly changing the nature of local reality or even draining its energies at the cost of destroying whatever is tapped. Fortunately however, although very potent, sorcery spells are usually limited in application.

corrections_applied: Normalized broken scan spacing and ligature artifacts.
Corrected apparent OCR artifact 'called are Folk Magic' to 'called Folk Magic'.

blocks.heading: Spirit Magic
blocks.text: A higher magical discipline, Spirit Magic is the practice of dealing with the spirit world and those spirits that are bound to the mortal realm in some form. Spirit magicians, or Animists, form relationships with particular spirits which work their supernatural effects on behalf of the magician or the community which they serve. Each spirit controls one or more specific effect(s), based upon natural processes of the world. All things, be they animate or inanimate, are possessed of a spirit — from the smallest of flowers to an entire mountain. The spirits of sapient creatures still bound to a mortal body are called souls.

blocks.heading: Lunar Magic
blocks.text: Last, but by no means least, is the odd and disturbing higher art of Lunar Magic. This type of magic gains its esoteric power from the Moon Rune, itself a strange facet of both Glamour and Chaos. In practice, those following the Lunar path are able to cheat the Great Compromise to call upon a wide array of powers, albeit at slightly less strength than other magical practitioners, depending on the phase of the Red Moon. However, those who dabble in Lunar Magic are prone to Illumination, and this often draws the hatred of other less open-minded cultures.

blocks.heading: Mysticism
blocks.text: The study of mysticism in Glorantha is limited to only those of Draconic blood or those deluded enough to become subservient to them. This however is a blessing in disguise, as Mysticism is perhaps the most potent of the magical arts, quite capable of radically and irrevocably altering the entire universe at the whim of the mystic, usually to the detriment of everyone else, since most practitioners believe all existence is merely an illusion that must be cast off before they can transcend to a higher state.

blocks.heading: Converting Older Source Material
blocks.text: Earlier editions of RuneQuest produced a great many different spells and spirits which no longer quite conform to the RuneQuest 6th Edition rules. Despite extensive work revising material from previous editions, converting cults and characters still requires some effort from the Games Master in order to redress both mechanical changes to the rules and conceptual changes to Glorantha itself.

To ease this process, the magic chapters either offer a reference to the revised spell in the RuneQuest 6th Edition Core Rules, or provide an entirely brand new version. It must be noted that in a few cases the changes are radical in nature, due to the prior reasons offered.

blocks.heading: General Rules for Gloranthan Magic
blocks.text: The following mechanics are offered merely as guidelines, and should not be treated as hard and fast rules if a Games Master wishes to retain an earlier play style in their game. For ease of description, the following section uses the word 'spells' by default, meaning spells, spirits or techniques, according to the type of magic utilised.

blocks.heading: Changes to Casting Skills
blocks.text: Since access to magic in Glorantha is via the Runes, the skills required to cast and manipulate some forms of magic have been revised to take into account the importance of Rune Affinities (see Chapter XX).

Taking Sorcery as an example, the skill of Invocation has been replaced with whichever Rune Affinity is linked to that spell, which in turn is further modified by the Four Principles (Combine/Separate, Command/Summon, Dismiss and Tap). Shaping, on the other hand, is replaced by the sorcerer's Law Rune affinity.
Careful study of these new rules will be required to avoid confusion.

blocks.heading: Spells Available per Temple or Teacher
blocks.text: Generally most cults, traditions or schools offer between 1d3+4 Folk Magic spells and another 1d3+4 advanced spells (Spirit Magic, Rune Magic, Sorcery, Lunar Magic or Mysticism) to their members.

corrections_applied: Normalized broken line breaks.
Regularized RuneQuest capitalization.

blocks.text: Not all cults are the same, however. Minor deities or hero cults might offer only a single additional spell or skill for those who desire to learn it, whereas the greatest gods or schools can offer a dozen or more.

blocks.heading: Availability of Spells
blocks.text: Generally only members of a cult, tradition or school are permitted to learn a spell belonging to that organisation. In addition, some spells are not available to members until a certain cult rank is achieved, to ensure they are not misused, or that cult secrets are not revealed to those uninitiated in the traditions of that organisation. There are always exceptions to the rules, however; for instance the cult of a trading deity might offer to exchange spell knowledge for other benefits.

blocks.heading: Price of Spells
blocks.text: Most Gloranthans must pay for the acquisition of new spells, despite their cult membership. Such knowledge is expensive, and can cost the equivalent of many months income to purchase. Folk Magic spells cost 50 Lunars (or 25 Lunars per point if using progressive Folk Magic), whilst higher magic such as Rune Magic or Sorcery costs 500 Lunars to learn each spell. Yet this financial value is only an abstraction, since the price might actually be paid for with a private gift, a votive sacrifice, or even as personal time offered in service to the organisation. Only rarely does it take the form of hard coinage.

blocks.heading: Learning Spells
blocks.text: Learning a spell in Glorantha is rarely just a case of being taught a simple magical formula which can be invoked ad hoc. Instead it is a matter of ritual and adherence to the tenets of that particular cult, tradition or school. However, just because a spell is available to the cult they belong to, it does not mean a character is permitted to learn it. Such tuition takes considerable time, not only the practice of the actual magic, but also the indoctrination of the student via stringent tasks or Heroquests.

Again, this can be abstracted to a mere investment of time and Experience Rolls for games in which learning is brushed over. On the other hand, the time requirement can be utilised to provide a period of adventure wherein the character performs objectives as part of discovering and mastering elements of the new spell.

blocks.heading: Casting Time
blocks.text: The casting time for all magic in Glorantha is one Action Point per Magic Point invested in the spell. A non-progressive Folk Magic spell for example will only take a single Action Point to cast, whereas a Rune Spell with a Magic Point cost of 3 will take three Action Points to complete.

blocks.heading: Running Out of Magic Points
blocks.text: When a character reduces their Magic Points to zero, they must succeed in a Willpower test or collapse insensate. If the cost of the spell is more than the caster's remaining Magic Points, the magic automatically fails. Whatever the specifics, until further Magic Points are recovered, no more magic can be cast.

blocks.heading: Recovering Magic Points
blocks.text: Glorantha is rich with magical energies, but when that energy recovers is dependent upon the mythic caveats of the God Time and personal runic affinities. Characters with an affinity to the Chaos, Darkness, Death or Disorder Runes replenish their full Magic Points with the setting of the sun. All others who lack those particular Runes replenish the entirety of their Magic Points at dawn.

blocks.heading: Restrictions on Casting
blocks.text: Magic requires freedom of movement and mind to be able to cast a spell. A caster suffers one additional difficulty grade to their casting skill for each of the following:

items: Mental Impairment. The caster is affected by magical mental or emotional domination, narcotic substances including sufficient alcohol, a Serious Wound to the head, or any other poisons and diseases that affect mental state.

uncertain_readings: The Experience Rolls values for Cantrip and Rune Spell are not confidently legible in the table.

Expanded hyphenated line breaks.

items: Physical Constriction. The caster is grappled, tied up or loses the use of one or more limbs.
Verbal Restraint. The caster cannot intone due to loss of tongue, wearing a gag or cannot breathe due to submersion or strangulation.

Suffers Pain. The concentration of the caster is shaken by suffering a Minor Wound. Serious Wounds inflict two difficulty grades, whilst Major Wounds make spell casting impossible due to incapacitation.

blocks.text: A spell automatically fails if the caster:

items: Ambulates at greater than walking speed.
Is unconscious or otherwise comatose.
Cannot see or sense the intended target of the spell, if appropriate.

blocks.text: There are no penalties for casting in close combat, as many spells are intended for battle. Games Masters may need to apply the above restrictions on a case by case basis according to the species or abilities of the caster.

blocks.heading: Stacking Magic
blocks.text: Magic of differing disciplines can be stacked together, provided that they are both available as part of the same cult, tradition or school the caster belongs to. In such a case the normal restriction, that only the magic with the greater Intensity take effect, is over-ruled. For example, an initiate of Humakt could combine a Bladesharp spell (Folk Magic) with a Truesword (Rune Magic) spell to both increase the default damage of his weapon and allow him to roll it twice.
Although stacking is permitted, it only applies to a single spell of each discipline. Thus the Folk Magic spells of Bladesharp and Fireblade couldn't be combined together with the Truesword spell.
In all other circumstances, the default rules concerning stacking takes precedence (see RuneQuest page 181).

blocks.heading: General Advice
blocks.text: The new magical rules presented in Adventures in Glorantha are intended for starting fresh campaigns using the RuneQuest 6th Edition rules as base. Game Masters converting over legacy games may find themselves more comfortable simply converting over effects of existing spells from previous versions of RuneQuest and avoid the mechanical changes necessary to readjust to the current view of Gloranthan practice.
In other words, if you are unhappy with the new way of doing things, then feel free to ignore the new rules as you wish. Your Glorantha Will Vary!

blocks.heading: There is No Game Balance!
blocks.text: Whether talking about the number of spells granted to a cult, or the disparate potency of its spells or spirits, one thing must be remembered — in Glorantha there is no Game Balance! Attempting to balance cults or spells is the antithesis of the mythological world. It is a place where gods can walk with men, Chaos monstrosities can flatten a city under one foot and even dragons are the size of mountain ranges.

blocks.heading: There is No Game Balance!
blocks.text: In real life, some organisations have more power and influence than others. It is no different in Glorantha. Orlanth, for example, gets all the coolest spells for adventuring. Such are the rewards of being the preeminent deity of Dragon Pass. If you want your character to have access to powerful magic, then he or she should join what most Gloranthan peoples do — choose wisely. It might not seem fair, but that's the way it is... live with it.

blocks.text: Folk Magic

The simplest and most straightforward of the magical approaches, folk magic can still be very powerful when used creatively. Every spell, no matter how basic, has its uses and a clever folk magician can work wonders with only a handful of charms.

Folk magic is the most basic form of magic, and available to just about every Gloranthan. Folk Magic is a set of common cantrips and minor magic effects that are used on an everyday basis to help with daily life, regular chores and, when the need calls for it, offensive and defensive purposes (and why Folk Magic is sometimes called Battle Magic by mercenary bands and martial cults).

Folk magic works here, now and on or against something that can be seen and felt. Its effects are temporary and generally not extreme (although this depends very much on the creativity of the caster and the circumstances for the spell). Folk Magic is not innate: no Gloranthan is born with a set of spells but the capacity to learn and work Folk Magic is as natural as learning to speak. Therefore, the majority of Gloranthans know a handful of Folk Magic spells and use them regularly and assume that everyone else uses them too. The very few who do not use Folk Magic, either because they have not been taught any, or for other reasons, are considered to be the exception and not the rule.

blocks.heading: Learning Folk Magic
blocks.text: Spells are taught by word of mouth, and passed down from generation to generation. They may be taught within a family, from a guild or even by wandering magicians. Anyone can learn and use a spell. There is no need to be able to read; the character simply memorises a series of words, gestures and mental symbols. However, if the character wishes to cast the spell at range, or to cast the spell on another living target, then they must create a focus through which the spell's energy will be channelled, usually involving the Rune from which the power is taken.

A focus can take many forms; Orlanthi prefer to tattoo the focus on their own bodies for example, but carving the focus into objects, weapons and armour are also popular choices amongst other cultures. If the focus is lost or damaged, the character will need to create a new one (hence the reason why tattoos and incised designs on the skin are a popular choice for creating foci). Although each focus is unique, it is possible to carve multiple foci for different spells into the same object. However, it is not possible to create multiple foci for the same spell; part of learning the spell consists of attuning the spiritual self to a specific focus.

blocks.heading: Casting Folk Magic
blocks.text: Folk Magic follows the usual RuneQuest rules, except that the casting is considered to be an Automatic Success (RuneQuest page 57) unless the spell is cast against a resisting target or is cast at range, in which case the Folk Magic skill must be rolled as normal. This may seem like a huge advantage compared to the RuneQuest default, but Glorantha is a world suffused with magic, and the ability to use minor folk magic is as normal to Gloranthans as breathing.

Casting a Folk Magic spell is rarely a silent or motionless activity. Every culture has its own ways of channelling natural magical energy, but chants, dances, drones, susurrations, ritual gesticulations, songs, rhymes and poems are frequent.

blocks.text: Freedom to move and make noise is essential; such activities align the runes in subtle but important ways, allowing Glorantha's abundant magical power to be swiftly harnessed and put to work.

blocks.heading: Folk Magic Spells
blocks.text: The following list contains legacy spells which have either been renamed, subtly changed in order to reflect the nature of Glorantha, or are unique to the world.

blocks.name: Binding
blocks.text: See Slow (RuneQuest page 193).

blocks.name: Brew Pratzim
blocks.text: This is now a cult skill, not a Folk Magic spell (see page XX).

blocks.name: Comprehension
blocks.text: This spell increases the target's mental capacity, so that the next INT-based skill check made by the recipient is one difficulty grade easier (provided they know that skill). INT-based skills are those which use INT to calculate their base value, such as Deceit, Perception or Seamanship.

blocks.name: Conceal Item
blocks.text: This spell renders invisible one or more items with a combined ENC value no greater than 3. Thus the caster could conceal a backpack full of tools, or that they are carrying around a forbidden weapon. The only time such objects become visible is if the caster openly interacts with it, for example if a concealed weapon is drawn and used to attack. In such cases, the object returns to obscurement once the interaction ceases.

blocks.name: Control (Species)
blocks.text: As per Pet (RuneQuest page 192), except that it can control a creature of SIZ up to twice the caster's CHA Characteristic.

blocks.name: Countermagic
blocks.text: This defensive spell creates an enduring magical shield, which blocks any Folk Magic spell cast at the recipient. It has no effect against Mysticism, Rune Magic, Sorcery or Spirit Magic.

blocks.name: Darkwall
blocks.text: Darkwall creates an area of shadow equal to the caster's POW in square metres (and a default height of one third of their POW), which suppresses all light within it. This is enough volume to fill a modest room, a length of corridor or form a small cloud if cast outside. All non-magical light, including sunlight, passing into or present within the boundary is reduced to the equivalent of a dim glow, so that it can be seen, but provides no illumination to see by. Other senses, such as sound, are unaffected by the shadow. A Darkwall can be moved or shaped into the depth or width desired, so long as the total area is equal to the caster's POW in square metres.

blocks.name: Delayed Ignite
blocks.text: Developed for some nefarious purpose by the Mostali, this spell works exactly like Ignite (RuneQuest page 191) except that it can only be cast on objects the user is touching. The object will then burst into flames anywhere from 1 Turn to five minutes later, at the caster's discretion. The spell can be cast upon an object someone is carrying or wearing, provided the user can touch the item.

blocks.name: Detect
blocks.text: See Find (RuneQuest page 190).

There are countless Detect spells and they work in the same way as Find X in the RuneQuest rules. Gloranthans, however, always use some form of focus or fetish for the spell (a wand of willow, a lodestone, a marble and so on) that offers a tangible indication of the subject's location. The focus guides the caster to the closest instance of the Detection subject within range (for example, Detect Enemy always has the focus point move towards anyone who knowingly intends to harm the caster).

Note that Detect spells can be very specific. A Detect Magic spell indicates the closest source of magic which, in a world like Glorantha, is not always very helpful. A Detect Sorcery spell though, will indicate the nearest sorcerer or sorcerous spell (assuming one is within range). Likewise there are separate spells for Detecting Iron, Gold, Chaos, the Undead, and so on.
Although generally very reliable, the Detect spell can be blocked by dense or thick materials such as metal, earth or stone at least one metre thick.

blocks.heading: Progressive Folk Magic
blocks.text: Veterans of RuneQuest and Glorantha will remember previous versions of Folk Magic with progressive spells that could exceed the limits presented in RuneQuest 6. There are good reasons for keeping Folk Magic as it is presented in RuneQuest 6 in order to differentiate it from higher magic. Should you wish to reintroduce progressive folk magic into your game, use the following optional rules.

blocks.heading: Progressive Folk Magic
blocks.text: A progressive spell can be cast at any Intensity up to or equal to half the caster's critical score with Folk Magic. So if a character knows Folk Magic at 65% then their critical score is 7%, allowing them to cast spells of up to Intensity 7. This value is capped to one third of the caster's INT; however, thus a caster with INT 17 is limited to a maximum Intensity of 6, even if they managed to increase their Folk Magic skill over 120%.

blocks.heading: Progressive Folk Magic
blocks.text: The cost of casting a Folk Magic spell is 1 Magic Point per Intensity. Unless otherwise stated, the Magnitude, casting time and other parameters of the spell do not change. However the precise effect of the extra Intensity varies by spell and is given in the list of progressive spells. These new interpretations supersede their non-variable versions in the core RuneQuest 6 rules.

blocks.heading: Progressive Folk Magic
blocks.text: Traditional Gloranthan spells that were omitted in RuneQuest 6 have now been included for the sake of completeness. It must be noted that some, such as Multimissile, will likely pose a degree of exceptional danger, but are partially balanced by their run cost in Magic Points. Finally, as a side-effect of becoming progressive, some spells actually end up being weaker at low Intensity than the standard version.

blocks.heading: Progressive Folk Magic
blocks.text: The Dice Bonus table is used with specific spells as noted in the Progressive Folk Magic Spells table.

blocks.title: Progressive Folk Magic Spells

rows.effect: Avert is used to dismiss another Folk Magic spell of equal or less Intensity within range.

Adds a bonus to the damage roll of an edged weapon as indicated on the Folk Magic Dice Bonus table. For example, Bladesharp 2 adds +1d2 damage, so a shortsword with Bladesharp 2 would roll 1d6+1d2 damage. Note that only the bonus is counted as magical damage for the purposes of harming certain creatures or spirits.
As per Bladesharp, except it applies only to bashing weapons. For example, Bludgeon 3 adds +1d3 damage (1d6 halved).

Creates a defensive shield about the recipient's body which guards against hostile magic. Each time they are magically assaulted, the recipient may roll the dice indicated by the Intensity of the Countermagic, and if the result is equal or higher than the Magnitude of the incoming spell, it is blocked.
This one-shot spell now inflicts 1d3 damage, plus an additional +1 per point of Intensity. For example, Disruption 3 would inflict 1d3+3 damage, at a cost of 3 Magic Points each time it was cast.
The spell's Intensity reduces the damage done as indicated on the Folk Magic Dice Bonus table, to a minimum of zero damage. Thus Dullblade 5 would reduce an edged weapon's rolled damage by 1d5.

Healing in Glorantha is more potent. The spell restores one point of damage per point of Intensity, but only when treating Minor and Serious Wounds. Otherwise it is the same as Heal (RuneQuest page 191).
As per Bladesharp, save it affects only natural weapons.
Each point of Intensity allows the caster to communicate with one person. The targets cannot communicate with each other directly, only via the caster who acts as a relay hub.
Each point of Intensity increases the walking, running and sprinting Movement Rates of the recipient by 1m. So at Intensity 1, the spell adds +1m; at Intensity 2 it adds +2m and so on.

Another one shot, instant spell, the recipient gains an additional missile per point of Intensity, in addition to the one they are shooting or throwing. Each magical missile flies toward a different target and must roll its attack separately. See the Multimissile entry on page 67 for more information.
Pierce ignores 1 Armour Point per point of Intensity.

blocks.name: Detection Blank
blocks.text: Casting this spell on an object, another person or oneself, temporarily renders the recipient undetectable by a Detect or Find spell. For example, a sorcerer carrying an iron dagger and wearing a gold ring would be immune to the spells Detect Sorcery, Detect Iron and Detect Gold.

blocks.name: Dispel Magic
blocks.text: See Avert (RuneQuest page 186).

blocks.name: Endurance
blocks.text: See Vigour (RuneQuest page 195).

blocks.name: Face of Lanbril
blocks.text: See Incognito (RuneQuest page 191).

blocks.name: False Healing
blocks.text: False Healing heals one Hit Location of choice, seemingly repairing all damage, stopping any bleeding and allowing it to return to functionality unless it was dismembered. However, the healing is merely illusory. Once the spell concludes, all the original damage returns, adding to any further damage it may have taken since — likely resulting in a Major Wound.

blocks.name: Farsee
blocks.text: See Magnify (RuneQuest page 192).

blocks.name: Food Song
blocks.text: Food Song ensures that plant and fertility deities such as Aldrya or Flamal are completely placated whenever the caster harvests vegetation for any reason (food, shelter, fire, weapons, and so on). It is more a courtesy spell than anything else, and is generally not used outside of elf forests.

blocks.name: Forget
blocks.text: This spell causes the target's memory to lapse for the spell's duration. The spell does not cause complete amnesia or permanent memory loss; rather it makes it difficult for the target to recall things of relative importance. Anyone affected by Forget must make a Formidable Insight roll to recall the details of any skill reliant on INT as one of its base characteristics, or to recall some important name, face, place, event or other fact.

blocks.name: Harmonize
blocks.text: A much weaker version of the lack of bear ability of the same name. Instead of being reduced to a complete puppet, the Harmonize spell causes the target to merely replicate the physical and verbal motions of the caster. The spell is broken if any action would cause physical harm to the target. While under the spell's effect, the target is aware that their actions are not their own, but is powerless to resist until the spell ceases.

blocks.name: Healing
blocks.text: See Heal (RuneQuest page 191). The spell is only effective on recipients linked to the Man Rune, such as humans, Aldryami, Mostali and Uz. It has no effect on other species.

blocks.name: Heat Metal
blocks.text: This spell causes one ENC of metal to heat up and become malleable. It takes several minutes for the metal to become red-hot, bringing it to the point where it can be smithed without need for a roaring fire or special fuel such as charcoal.

blocks.name: Hotfoot
blocks.text: If the target fails to resist this spell, he or she receives a burning pain in one foot (chosen by the caster), and cannot stand on that foot for the rest of the melee round. Assuming that they are bipedal this will result in them falling over, unless the target succeeds in an Athletics roll, allowing them to hop around on their other foot until the heat subsides.

blocks.name: Ignite Flesh
blocks.text: If the target of this spell fails to resist, a small Intensity 1 fire will be ignited on the flesh of a random Hit Location. The flames can be extinguished normally by taking a full round to smother them. Creatures naturally resistant to fire are immune to this spell.

blocks.name: Invisibility
blocks.text: See Incognito (RuneQuest page 191). However, instead of becoming bland and unmemorable, Invisibility draws observers' attention away from the recipient. The spell immediately ceases if the recipient causes some sort of ruckus, or launches an attack on another with either spells or weapons.

uncertain_readings: The phrase 'lack of bear ability' in Harmonize is visually ambiguous and may be a setting term or scan-degraded phrase.
corrections_applied: Normalized broken words and references.
Preserved ambiguous phrase in Harmonize rather than silently substituting.

blocks.name: Jumping
blocks.text: The Jump spell increases the range one can jump when using an Athletics roll (see RuneQuest page 58). With the Jump spell cast, one can leap up to five times one's own height horizontally, or up to three times one's own height vertically. When using the Jump spell, a run-up is not needed; the same distances can be accomplished from a standing start.

blocks.name: Lantern
blocks.text: See Light (RuneQuest page 191), but lasts until the following dawn.

blocks.name: Lightwall
blocks.text: This spell creates a wall of light which has a surface area (length times height) equal to the caster's POW in metres. The wall presents a blinding glare on one side which is visually impenetrable, while from the other side it is transparent and can be seen through. Furthermore, the glow illuminates both sides like a Light spell. It may be moved by the caster at a rate of one metre per strike rank, and it can be specified to take any form, provided it does not exceed its maximum dimensions.

blocks.name: Multimissile
blocks.text: This spell should only be used if Progressive magic is allowed. Each point of the spell cast upon a ranged weapon projectile creates a duplicate magical missile the instant that the original is loosed or thrown. All missiles, original and magical, do the same amount of damage; however, each of the missile attacks is rolled separately and flies towards a separate target. For example, a caster who knows Multimissile 3 could launch a volley of arrows from his bow against four broo: 1 original plus 3 magical missiles, each of which must strike a different target. Note that this spell only works on personal weapons, not siege engines.

blocks.name: Pamalt's Touch
blocks.text: This spell increases the recipient's resistance to other Folk Magic spells by one grade.

blocks.name: Parry
blocks.text: The Parry spell is cast on a particular weapon and it increases that weapon's size, for parrying purposes, by one step. Thus, a dagger with size Small (S) becomes size Medium (M), and a shield with size Huge (H) would become size Colossal (C). Weapons of size C and above are unaffected by the spell.

blocks.name: Peaceful Cut
blocks.text: This is now a cult skill, not a Folk Magic spell (see Waha page XX).

blocks.name: Preserve Herbs
blocks.text: See Preserve (RuneQuest page 193), save that it only affects plant matter.

blocks.name: Protection
blocks.text: Protection adds a layer of physical protection worth 2 Armour Points over the recipient's entire body. This does not stack atop worn armour, but may supersede it if the spell's protective value is greater.

blocks.name: River Eyes
blocks.text: This spell permits the recipient to see clearly through the murky waters of lakes and rivers for a distance equal to the caster's POW as if gazing through air. It also removes all refractions and reflection when looking out of the water at objects or creatures above the surface.

blocks.name: Second Sight
blocks.text: As per Witchsight (RuneQuest page 195), but only allows the caster to see active magic, enchanted items and the magical aura (POW) of those visible to the caster's normal sight. The strength of an aura should be described in relative terms rather than hard numbers, using the accompanying table.

corrections_applied: Expanded broken line breaks and spacing.
Normalized spell trait punctuation.

blocks.name: Seek Sun Dome Temple
blocks.text: This spell causes a gleam of golden light to flash on the horizon in the exact direction of the nearest Sun Dome Temple. The gleam is visible only to the caster.

blocks.name: Shimmer
blocks.text: This spell blurs and distorts the target's visual image, making it harder to hit. Anyone attempting to strike the subject of a Shimmer spell must make a successful Perception roll directly before the attack in order to discern the underlying shape. If the Perception roll is either not made or failed, then attacks are one grade more difficult. Note that the Perception roll does not count as a separate action during a combat round; it is assumed to form part of the attack itself.

blocks.name: Silence
blocks.text: Silence muffles incidental noises of movement made by the recipient (the brush of clothing, soft footsteps, etc.), allowing them to make Stealth attempts one grade easier. It will not stop someone from chanting a spell, issuing commands, or raising the alarm if you are detected.

blocks.name: Sneeze
blocks.text: The caster must overcome the Willpower of the target. If successful, the target begins sneezing the strike rank following casting, and continues sneezing for their next 1d3 Turns. During the time spent sneezing the target is nearly incapacitated, and can only parry or evade at a Hard difficulty. If the target was casting a spell and fails to resist, the spell is immediately disrupted.

blocks.name: Solace
blocks.text: This spell is cast upon a sufferer. It acts to cancel mental distress, whether from grief over the loss of a loved one, from fear of death, or whatever. The effect is to relieve stress on the victim and enable him or her to go about normal actions. It does not relieve pain, such as from injury or disease, but it does enable him to face the prospect of death bravely.

blocks.name: Speedart
blocks.text: Speedart boosts the velocity of thrown or fired missiles so that they gain a +2 damage bonus and increase both Close and Effective Range by 50%.

blocks.name: Spirit Binding
blocks.text: This spell is now superseded by the inherent abilities of Shamans (page 134) and Gifts given by Rune Cults (page XX).

blocks.name: Spiritshield
blocks.text: This spell creates a defence for a recipient engaged in Spirit Combat. Every time the character takes spirit damage, they may reduce the number of Magic Points lost by 2 points.

blocks.name: Strength
blocks.text: See Might (RuneQuest page 192).

blocks.name: Strong Hammer
blocks.text: This spell temporarily makes a crafting tool capable of working materials that ordinarily it would not affect or that would irreparably damage it. For example, a smith might use the spell on a small hammer to cold-work bronze, or crack stone. The spell has no effect on the damage inflicted by the tool, nor the skill of its user. It merely prevents the item from suffering breakage (see RuneQuest page 123).

blocks.name: Visibility
blocks.text: As per Witchsight (RuneQuest page 195), but only allows the caster to see discorporated spirits, shapeshifted creatures and penetrate illusions.

blocks.name: Xenohealing
blocks.text: See Heal (RuneQuest page 191), but applies to creatures not linked to the Man Rune.

blocks.text: Rune Magic

The Storm Rune, representative of the Orlanthi culture. They place a premium on bravery, generosity, and honour; their first sacred law is "No One Can Make You Do Anything", the second: "There is Always Another Way".

In Glorantha, the gods and their manifestations are real. Save perhaps for True Dragons, Gods are the most potent beings in the universe. There is a hierarchy among deities, ranging from Great Gods such as Humakt down to tribal ancestors, transcended heroes and still-mortal demigods. Any of these may be the object of a Rune Cult — though a dominant god can lend far more power to its initiates than a weak one can. Worship of a god consists of sacrifices to strengthen the deity, in return for guarantees that the god will deliver its magic when promised.

blocks.heading: Rune Cults
blocks.text: A Rune Cult is an association of individuals who gather together, providing worship or propitiation to a particular deity. The cult maintains the ritual and responsibilities for its god, offering up sacrifices and caring for its shrine. Each cult possesses a priesthood that officiates at sacred rituals and acts as the intermediaries between the cult members and the god.

Cults are usually constrained to particular regions or peoples, their temples located at sacred sites where the deity was active during the God Time. A single god may have several different cults dedicated to it. However, not all gods have cults; and some are found only at a single location.

Cults may be divided into three classes, depending on how widely worshipped the god is, and how powerful it is. There are actually several interrelated factors, as a god draws its power from being worshipped.

The first and most powerful are cults devoted to one of the great gods. These deities are the owners of particular Runes, and are known everywhere in Glorantha, sometimes by other names. Examples of this sort of cult are the cults of Ernalda and Orlanth. A major cult will have hundreds of thousands of worshippers across the world, and a large hierarchy with many temples, most of which contain shrines to associated deities, spirits and cult heroes.

The second type of deity has tens of thousands of worshippers, of which cults like Babeester Gor or Humakt are prime examples. Whilst they still possess enough popularity to establish and maintain temples, their modest size usually precludes shrines to associated gods. What shrines do exist are limited to their own cult spirits and heroes.

The third class of cult is composed of minor deities, whose combined worshippers rarely rise to the thousands. These offer propitiation to founding ancestors, subsidiary cult heroes and powerful spirits who have not managed to make the jump to godhood. Minor cults never own their own temples, and indeed often have no actual priesthood. Lanbril is an example of a minor cult. The personality cults of still living demigods can be counted amongst these.

blocks.heading: Temples
blocks.text: A temple is a holy place, consecrated and protected by a god or gods. They typically have their own guardian entity and sacred objects. Temples are supported by the local community, being an important source of specialised magical resources. Not all temples require a building; many Orlanthi temples for example are natural places that provide access to

corrections_applied: Corrected broken body text and ligature artifacts.
Preserved the page break in the final temple sentence.

blocks.text: the Otherworld, such as hilltops, notable trees or standing stones. Some are even mobile like the wheeled oxcarts of Lokarnos that travel from place to place.

Most temples are served by full-time priests assigned to the sacred site, placing themselves at the behest of worshippers. Shrines are similar to temples save that they are smaller, often lacking a dedicated priest to maintain them. But in return, they offer access to lesser known magics, due to some mythic deed performed on that specific spot.

blocks.heading: Priests
blocks.text: Priests are people with the full-time occupation of leading magical activities. In Glorantha, the title of Priest is a profession, rather than a cult rank. They are responsible for maintaining cult holy places and regalia, performing sacrifices and key rituals, requesting divinations and organizing the support of the cult.

All priests are important people in the community, sustained by their cult members. Some priests owe their position to their devotion or magical affinities, others to their knowledge of cult rituals and myths, still others to their bloodline or reputation. They are fully supported by their local community, being given land, herds and some portion of all sacrifices made to the gods. Because of this, priests are in a position to devote their lives fully to their god if they wish.

blocks.heading: Rune Cult Structure
blocks.text: Gloranthan cults possess a universal hierarchy which divides their worshippers into three broad groups, as described in the following sections: Lay Members, Initiates and Devotees. Despite this apparent ubiquity, cults have their own rank titles, membership requirements and duties which can vary wildly.

blocks.heading: Staves from the Storm Priest

sections.question: Where did the world come from?
sections.answer: Dead lie world and Umath the Free broke the sky's curse and moved the world. Umath roared and shall remain, set the world in separate ways. Now all feel the world's winds at their backs.

sections.question: Where did I come from?
sections.answer: Humans are wind-born, free of fire. Orlanth breathed your first life into you. Follow the winds, like Orlanth before you. Yours is the path-choice: make it and live.

sections.question: Why do we die?
sections.answer: Stale the world was, unchanging and solid. No one would die, so no one could live. Orlanth and Humakt, brothers in bravery, brought Death to the world and gave life an edge. Sure live the innocent who nothing plagued are. First of them Lorionge, who bends us to solah. Now there is Death, who prompts us to live life. Slayer of foes who has no respite to friends.

sections.question: What happens after we die?
sections.answer: Death is a wonder, a rest for our march. Orlanth found it, and fought it in turn. Defeated Death falls, and brings the Sun out. Fulfilled is their Quest, the Lightbringer's glory. Death is the endless guide to last journey. Carries you safe to the God's Brazen Hall. There presides Orlanth, his table for loyal thanes. Tell him your stories, take your rightful place.

sections.question: Why am I here?
sections.answer: Life is for living, too, to your fullest. Challenge the challenges, carve out your place. Be faithful to friends, relentless to foes, loving to kith, and fulfil your wyrd.

sections.question: How do I do magic?
sections.answer: Great among gifts, storm tools gave magic. Tough men to priests, first among followers. Filled with breath, we become one with Orlanth. Might lets all men work with the world.

sections.question: What are spirits?
sections.answer: Great were the gods who fought Orlanth's gifts. Many were broken or made too small. Now the survivors are spirits for bold men. Shamans evoke their small magic charms. Notice and praise these spirits sundered. Some can be helpful, perform small deeds. Do not give them worship; keep from them your audience. That is for Orlanth King alone, their conqueror and liege.

sections.question: What is sorcery?
sections.answer: Woe to the brisk who try without gods. Their souls sink slowly, fall empty to hell.

blocks.heading: Lay Members
blocks.text: Most cults allow people to worship without pledging themselves to the god or learning its inner secrets. Such worshipers are referred to as Lay Members. There are no restrictions to joining as a Lay Member provided the character is in good standing within the community and none of their Rune Affinities are in direct opposition to those of the god. Thus it is not unusual to be a Lay Member of several cults. For example, even the most devout Humakti might take the opportunity to attend an Mastakos charioteer festival if it offers itself. But the warrior will be forbidden to join the cult of Chalana Arroy as a Lay Member, because the goddess's Life Rune is the antithesis of Humakt's Death Rune.

Cults will often offer substantial mundane benefits to its Lay Members such as shelter, protection, legal representation, training, healing and so on. Yet there is no magical benefit to being a Lay Member beyond being able to learn its Folk Magic, or request that the cult priests cast a Rune Spell on your behalf.

In return, the lay member is expected, but not forced, to attend cult ceremonies held each seasonal Holy Day and make an offering of Magic Points to that deity. In addition they might be asked to contribute to the maintenance of the local shrine or temple, which can take the form of small jobs, material donations or a minor tithe of money.

blocks.heading: Initiates
blocks.text: A character who shares at least one Rune with a god, and meets any other cult requirements, can become an initiate of that god's cult. Initiation into a cult is a serious step, for initiates pledge themselves to the focus of a divine entity. They must swear to follow the commands of the god as expressed by the priests of the cult, participate in the cult Holy Day ceremonies, and are expected to commit a significant proportion of their time and attention to that god alone. Characters can start out as an initiate if they possess at least five of the cult skills at 50% or better; one of which must be a Rune Affinity of the deity.

Initiates develop a personal divine connection with the deity, performing in its myths and rituals to gain access to greater magic than common lay worshippers. As an initiate, the character creates a Devotional Pool with that specific god through which they donate Magic Points to 'feed' the deity, and in return the character is allowed to call on its Rune Magic (RuneQuest page 260). To account for the restricted number of cult ranks, a Gloranthan initiate may place a number of Magic Points up to a maximum of half their POW Characteristic into the Devotional Pool.

Many gods can be approached via more than one Rune. For example, Orlanth can be contacted via the Air, Movement and Mastery Runes — each forming a separate aspect of Orlanth. Thus, someone who approaches Orlanth from the Movement Rune specialises in the Adventurous aspect of Orlanth. However, the initiate can still call upon Orlanth's Air and Mastery powers providing the character has those runic affinities.

Since the connection with a deity is based upon their runic bonds, the skill of Exhort is now replaced by whatever Rune Affinity controls the particular spell being cast. So an initiate of Yelmalio who wished to cast a Sun Spear would need to roll against their Fire Rune affinity to call down the Rune Spell successfully. If the Yelmalio worshipper lacked the Fire Rune affinity, then they would be unable to call upon the spell at all.
The Devotion skill is still used to judge the Intensity and Magnitude of Rune Spells being cast. It may also be used as:

items: A personality trait, to reflect the character's closeness to the personality of his or her god, as indicated by the cult description.
A knowledge ability, to recount knowledge of the temples, rituals and other lore of the cult, as well as the god's mythic conflicts with other deities or spirits.
A flaw, as used by the Game Master to hinder the character when attempting to act in a manner contrary to the ethos of the god, or cult obligations.

blocks.text: The virtues, flaws and cult special abilities gained from Rune Affinities will vary from local cult to local cult. A character may be an initiate of multiple deities, but only to mutually associated and friendly cults, and only when their Runes are compatible — you cannot initiate both to the honourable Death god Humakt and to the deceitful thieves' cult of Lanbril!

Keep in mind that each cult requires a significant contribution of your time, becoming initiated into two or more cults may end up forcing the character to spend most of their available time in mandatory cult activities and worship. In addition, despite having access to multiple Devotional Pools, the total number of Magic Points concurrently invested across multiple pools still cannot exceed half the character's POW.

For example Horus the Hairy is an initiate of Humakt and being somewhat smarter than most adventurers, he is also an initiate of Lhankor Mhy, using their archives to research areas for treasure hunting. With

blocks.text: his POW of 15 he can place a maximum of 8 Magic Points into either of his two Devotional Pools. However, he cannot invest 8 into both simultaneously as this would exceed the limit of half his POW. So in preparation for another expedition into the Big Rubble, he decides to put 6 MP into his Humakt pool and just 2 MP into his Lhankor Mhy pool, hoping this time that fool Rurik won't get him into trouble...

blocks.heading: Limitations on Cult Rune Magic
blocks.text: As a cult initiate, a character may directly use a Rune Affinity shared with his god to cast Rune Spells, relying upon the power of that god to create the magic. However, the spells available to the cult are limited to those utilised by the god in its myths, and are subject to any restrictions that the god himself has.

The Air Rune, for instance, is associated with Air, breath, violence, weather, storm and wind. Orlanth, the owner of the Air Rune, grants the broadest range of Air magic to his worshippers — even enabling them to fly with the winds! In contrast, Valind is the god of Winter and can use his Air Rune only to create snow, wind and ice.

The cult write-up for each god (see page XX) gives the Rune Spells available to it. To enable more flexibility in designing cults and their powers, the list of Rune Spells later in this chapter has been stripped of specific cult associations, and replaced instead with the Runic Affinity controlling that power. This should allow Game Masters more latitude when converting cults over from previous versions of RuneQuest, or creating their own. After all, Your Glorantha Will Vary.

blocks.heading: Acting Like Your God
blocks.text: An initiate's fidelity to his or her god has a great impact on personality. Game Masters are encouraged to permit characters to augment skills with their Devotion (see RuneQuest, page 76), in situations where they act in a manner in keeping with the god's personality. Similarly, the Game Master should freely assign penalties against skill checks when a character has not been acting like their deity.

At times it is possible that the god will compel an initiate to do things without being fully aware of what he or she is doing. The character may try to resist, but at the cost of permanently reducing their Devotion skill by 1d6+4%. This compulsion tends to occur during religious rituals, on cult Holy Days, and at places of magical power.

After drinking one beer too many, Horus decides to paint 'Lunars Go Home!' on the walls of the Seven Mothers temple. Unfortunately he is seen, followed back through the streets of Pavis to the Halls of Humakt. Later the priest summons him before the shrine of the god and asks whether Horus has any knowledge of the affair. Realising the trouble this might cause the cult with the Lunar authorities, Horus tries to lie, forcing him to make an opposed roll of his Deceit against Devotion (Humakt), the power of the god compelling him to tell the truth, no matter the consequences. Somehow the Deceit roll wins, and Horus manages to bluff his innocence. Yet as he does so, he feels the cold disapproval of Humakt and his Devotion skill is permanently lowered by 7%...

blocks.heading: Devotees
blocks.text: A character who wishes to be a devotee must be willing to dedicate their life solely to the service of the chosen deity, abandoning all other magic that does not come from that god, its subservient cult heroes or gifted by associated cults. To become a devotee is not easy; the character must pass difficult tests given not just by the temple hierarchy, but also by the deity itself. They are tested on their piety, knowledge of the deity's myths and their personal virtues. Characters only qualify for the chance to undertake these stringent challenges if they possess at least three of the cult skills at 100% or better, one of which must be a Rune Affinity they share with the deity.

If the character is accepted, they are marked by the god, gifted with special powers, and have limits imposed on their behaviour. The devotee must renounce initiate status to any other deities not directly related to that cult. They may still perform Folk Magic learned from other cults, but can no longer use any Rune Magic not already available to them via associate cults. In addition, the character must also permanently lose the ratings and abilities of any spirit magic or wizardry they may have held. Obviously, a character can only devote themselves to one cult, but in return are granted high status and honorific titles such as Rune Lord or Rune Priest. To compensate for this utter dedication, the devotee may increase the maximum number of Magic Points placed in their Devotional Pool to a value equal to their POW.

blocks.heading: Feats
blocks.text: A feat is a magical cult gift which emulates one of the worshipped god's mythic deeds, whose scope is beyond even that of the most potent Rune Spells. When a character becomes a devotee, they are granted one feat automatically as part of their inauguration, and can learn additional feats by performing significant Heroquests (see page XX for more information on Heroquests), and spending a suitable number of Experience Rolls to master that ability, providing the deity in question exhibited said power during the time of myths.

corrections_applied: Merged example text split across pages.
Normalized quote marks and RuneQuest capitalization.

blocks.text: Using a feat literally channels the full power of the god or the myths associated with him. Invoking a feat is not an invisible act. The devotee always exhibits some form of manifestation of the magical powers at his disposal; they might appear to grow larger, burn with an inner glow, crackle lightning from their fingertips, or even start to physically resemble the cult images of the deity. This is known as incarnating, whereby the devotee becomes their god.

To incarnate the god requires the character to roll against whichever Rune Affinity is associated with that feat. This normally requires several days of ritual preparation, but can be rushed if the character accepts a difficulty penalty of Hard to reduce it to hours or Formidable to make it mere minutes. If successful the character may use the feat for any activity within its scope; but once invoked, the feat must be ended before actions may be taken outside of it. As long the actions in the myth are followed, the feat endures.

When a character performs the feats of their god, they must act as the god mythically did or else weaken the magic. This is sometimes called an identity challenge — when the character is forced to choose between acting as the player wants or as the god did. If the character insists on acting in a manner contrary to the myth the Game Master may force them to roll over the Rune Affinity in question. If successful the character may act as wished — but the Rune Affinity will be permanently lowered by 1d6+4%.

blocks.heading: Creating New Feats
blocks.text: New feats can be 'discovered' by Heroquesting and uncovering truths long forgotten since the Great Compromise. Such quests are incredibly dangerous, but the rewards of finding new feats are invaluable, not only expanding the myths of a cult but also making the successful devotee the focus of a new sub-cult. Examples include heroes such as Gore & Gash, or Kalikos.

blocks.heading: Leaving a Rune Cult
blocks.text: Leaving a cult as an initiate or devotee is not easy and requires permission from the priesthood. If not granted or, worse still, they are cast out for transgressing cult tenets, this results in a magical attack from the cult's spirit(s) of reprisal. Even if the apostate survives the attack, he remains the enemy of that god and often of its cult worshippers.

blocks.heading: Cult Holy Days and High Holy Days
blocks.text: Gloranthan cults draw together their worshippers on seasonal Holy Days and yearly High Holy Days, to make sacrifices of both material goods and Magic Points to their god. This worship has several benefits. First and foremost it allows worshippers to re-enact the myths of the god, potentially allowing HeroQuests to occur. Secondly, the deity grants some of the power gathered from its worshippers towards 'free' castings of any Rune Spell an initiate or devotee knows.

In many cases it is used frivolously to augment the celebrations, such as to summon good weather or awe the lay members with magical displays. Some priests use this gift more wisely, to power long-term enchantments such as Bless Woad or Fertilize, so that they need not be maintained from the Devotional Pool of the caster. Most often this gift is used to renew the Sanctify spell on the cult temple or shrine, plus any other Rune Spells the priesthood wishes to embed within it.

Holy days provide each initiate and devotee in attendance with a single Rune Spell of 1 Magic Point which must be cast during the ritual festivities. High Holy Days on the other hand provide one Rune Spell of up to 3 Magic Points. A worshipper whose 'free' Rune Spell is functioning when the next Holy or High Holy Day comes round, cannot receive a new one.

blocks.heading: Subservient Cults
blocks.text: Many cults include the worship of minor spirits and dead heroes linked to its god, who would not otherwise be venerated or even remembered. Subservient spirits are those subdued or befriended by the god before time began and now serve out of obligation. Examples of this are Spirits of Reprisal who punish those that dare to abandon the cult, or transgress its rules. Cult heroes on the other hand were once mortals who developed new powers or skills in devotion to their deity.

Save for Spirits of Reprisal which remain at the beck and call of the deity itself, contact with subservient cults are almost always limited to a few shrines or an individual temple usually within the same geographical region. For instance, the Humakt subservient cult of Hiia One Armed has a single shrine located in the Grazelands within Dragon Pass. Those Humakt worshippers who wish to learn the unbreakable blade magic of Hiia must first undertake a quest to this shrine in order to prove their worth and sacrifice for it.

corrections_applied: Expanded line-break artifacts.
Normalized HeroQuests/Heroquesting as visibly styled on the page where possible.

blocks.text: Once the spirit, skill or spell of the subservient cult has been learned, a worshipper is expected to return once per year in order to either pay their respects, or pass on the knowledge to others who come to the shrine seeking it. Any Rune Spell learned from a sub-cult is powered by the Devotional Pool of the overarching cult.

blocks.heading: Associated Cults
blocks.text: Associated cults are those which have formed an alliance or relationship with the main cult. Within a pantheon gods often lend their aid to each other's worshippers, in gratitude or recompense for deeds performed during the God Time. Like subservient cults, this help can take myriad form, from knowledge of a skill to an alliance with a species of animal. In most cases however, the associated cult provides a Rune Spell that the worshipper can only utilise if they share the same Rune Affinity.

Gaining access to the spell requires the worshipper to undertake a pilgrimage to a shrine or temple of the associated god, then make an appropriate sacrifice. Provided no offense has been given the god gifts the supplicant a single use of its loaned spell, reducing the requester's own cult Devotional Pool by the requisite number of Magic Points normally used to cast that spell. Once the gifted Rune Spell is cast these points are freed, but the worshipper is forced to make a new sacrifice at a holy place of the associated cult before the spell may be utilised again.

Due to their divine fraternal relationship, Sathril a unicorn rider of Yelorna is permitted to request the Catseye spell from the cult of Yelmalio. In order to gain this Rune Magic, she must first visit the nearest Sun Dome temple and make an offering to Yelmalio — usually a yellow feathered bird or a golden trinket. If she is not found wanting, Yelmalio allows Sathril one-use of his power which reduces her Devotional Pool with Yelorna by 1 Magic Point. After it has been cast her pool returns to its normal size, but Sathril cannot use Catseye again until she returns to a Sun Dome temple and sacrifices for its loan once more.

blocks.heading: Rune Spells
blocks.text: The following section provides updated and sometimes heavily modified versions of Rune Spells originally found in RuneQuest 2nd and 3rd edition publications such as Cults of Prax, Cults of Terror and Gods of Glorantha. During the course of RuneQuest history, innumerable Rune Spells have been created to grant individuality to literally hundreds of divine cults.

To conserve space, only minimal details concerning the mechanical effect of the spells are provided. Other information such as the original cults which provided them has been, where possible, deliberately left out so as to rededicate the magic with more general runic associations. In this way it should be easy to rebuild legacy cults not covered by Adventures in Glorantha or create brand new ones. If a spell's runic association now makes it unavailable to a cult which once provided it, Game Masters should feel free to ignore the limitation or expand its runes as necessary.

Gloranthan spells which are already part of the RuneQuest 6th Edition rules are listed with the page number where that spell can be found, along with its runic association and any changes to its traits. Remember that spells with a duration of Days, Weeks or Months must be maintained by the caster, tying up the Devotional Pool points used to cast them until they expire.

Note that as Rank has been reduced in importance, a new parameter has been added called Cost, which is the number of Magic Points required to cast the spell from the user's Devotional Pool. In cases where the spell is granted from an associated cult, this default cost may be increased.

blocks.heading: All Cults Are Equal...
blocks.text: But some cults are more equal than others. Across Glorantha the power and influence of divine cults varies considerably. This is not only true in terms of congregation size, political standing and economics, but also in what magic is made available to their worshippers. Some Gloranthan Rune Spells can be near useless to an adventurer but wield tremendous importance to a community. Neither do all cults provide the same number of Rune spells to their members. Players new to RuneQuest may view this as 'unfair' or 'broken'. But in Glorantha there is no game balance, at least not at the mechanical level. Instead there are sociological obligations and consequences which limit what an individual is permitted to do. A character who is an initiate of Humakt can slay another with a wave of his hand. At face value he may seem far more powerful than the local Ernalda priestess who can only bless the fields and guarantee pregnancy; but in reality the importance of the priestess far surpasses the Humakti and at times of need the entire village or town will rally to her call, for the long-term survival of their family and friends depends on her.

blocks.heading: All Cults Are Equal...
blocks.text: The secret of playing in Glorantha is to realise that campaigns can be far more than just endless adventures killing and looting. Glorantha emphasises the importance of cult and community, and clever players can easily become the movers and shakers of a campaign as members of cults with armed with what appears on paper to be the most feeble of Rune spells.

blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Rank Initiate, Runes [Infinity glyph, second rune glyph unclear]

blocks.name: Accelerate Growth
blocks.stat_line: Area (Metres), Cost 1, Duration (Minutes), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: Accelerate Growth accelerates the growth of vegetation, ageing it by a number of years equal to the Intensity of the spell. Under its effects, all plants in the area increase in size and verdure dramatically, although they cannot grow beyond their normal maximum size. The growth occurs over the course of several minutes, but remains permanent once the spell ends. The spell is normally used to establish orchards or rapidly spread forests, at the cost of the sprawling, tangled proliferation within the area of effect. Repeated use of this miracle can potentially grow a thick forest over what had been open fields a few days previously.

blocks.name: Affix Darkness
blocks.stat_line: Area (Metres), Cost 1, Duration (Special), Rank Initiate, Runes [Darkness glyph]
blocks.text: Affix Darkness must be cast at night, and lasts until the following sunset. It preserves the darkness in a volume up to the full area of the spell, depending on the location and desires of the caster. For instance, in the open the spell usually occupies a hemisphere with a radius equal to the Intensity in metres; whereas if cast inside a building, the caster could limit its extent to within the walls and no further. The affixed darkness remains exactly as dark as when the spell was first cast. Bringing a torch or other light source inside the area illuminates it as normal.

blocks.name: Alter Creature
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Hours), Rank Initiate, Resist (Willpower), Ranged (Touch), Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: When cast upon a creature or person subject to the Survival Covenant of Waha, it reverses their status: herd beasts become people and people become herd beasts. They do not change physically. A beast which becomes a person may convert their INS to INT and may roll 3d6 for CHA. They become omnivorous, preserve their previous skills and start with a Native Tongue skill of INT+CHA. On the other hand, when a person is reduced to the status of a beast, their INT is transformed to INS, they lose their CHA characteristic and all skills other than Athletics, Brawn, Endurance, Evade, Perception, Stealth, Swim, Unarmed and Willpower. No language remains to them, but they can graze upon vegetation and survive. Normally this spell is used to temporarily awaken a favoured herd beast to aid in a task, or to chastise people who have transgressed cult tenets. If the spell endures long enough, from application of a Feat or via the Rune Spell of Extension for instance, an awakened beast may learn other skills.

blocks.name: Alter Spirit of Disease
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Instant), Rank Initiate, Ranged (Metres), Resist (Willpower), Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: Alter Spirit of Disease must be cast upon a disease spirit prior to its infestation of a victim. It permits the caster to permanently change the disease conditions according to the Intensity of the spell (see Disease Condition table page 81). Note that this spell can be used to strengthen or mediate a disease spirit's conditions, potentially weakening such spirits if faced as guardians, or ruining a spirit combat prior to a victim succumbing. If the spirit is hostile to the caster, it gains a chance to resist the magic.

blocks.name: Analyze Magic
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Minutes), Ranged (Metres), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell reveals one or more truths concerning the function of a single magical item, entity or substance. The number of questions the caster may ask is equal to the Intensity of the spell, but must be used before the spell expires.

blocks.name: Animate War Tree
blocks.text: See Create Warrior of Wood, page 88.

blocks.name: Appease (Deity)
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Area (Special), Duration (Weeks), Rank Initiate, Runes Special
blocks.text: Appease is a ritual utilised to perform sacrifices to dangerous gods or spirits to placate them, as per the Propitiate spell (RuneQuest page 273). There are a myriad of different versions of this spell, and the required sacrifices depend upon the particular cult. For example, the Cult of the Bloody Tusk accepts copious amounts of grain, freshly spilled blood or magical items as valid sacrifices, to ensure that their giant boars remain tame and under tusk rider control. If the caster rolls a critical success on his casting roll, whoever provided the sacrifice is granted a temporary bonus to their lands,

blocks.name: Appease (Deity)
blocks.text: beasts or personal safety, provided they are within the area of the spell. In the case of Bloody Tusk, a bonus of half the spell's Intensity can be applied to a single characteristic, up to the species maximum.

blocks.name: Arrow Trance
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Rounds), Ranged (Touch), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell merges the caster's consciousness with their bow, augmenting their ranged Combat Style to unprecedented levels by boosting the Level of Success of any bow attack by one step. Thus a Fumble upgrades to a Fail, a Fail to a Success and a Success to a Critical; rolled Critical Successes cannot be boosted higher. Whilst under its influence, the user enters a trance in which only the bow and targets exist; only moving to establish a clear shot or find another enemy. They cannot wield any other weapon, parry, evade, use their bow as a club or even cast other magic, including healing. If the battle ends before the spell expires, the caster will stand at rest and wait for new targets.

blocks.name: Asrelia's Cavern
blocks.stat_line: Area (Metres), Cost 3, Duration (Hours), Rank Priest, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: Asrelia's Cavern creates an invisible barrier, forming a sphere centring about the point of casting. Wind cannot blow through the barrier, rain and snow cannot penetrate it, and it blocks flames. Even a flood washes up against the barrier harmlessly. Spells can be cast through the invisible boundary, and creatures can cross it freely. Only non-living natural forces are barred entrance. Elementals can still breach it.

blocks.name: Attack Soul
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Rounds), Ranged (Metres), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell enables the caster to attack a single chosen target as if they were engaged in spirit combat. If the caster overcomes the target's Willpower, the target loses a number of Magic Points according to the caster's Devotion skill (see Spirit Damage table, RuneQuest page 201). Just as in normal spirit combat, the target can resist and attack back, reducing the caster's Magic Points. Unless one of the participants discorporates or is capable of discorporating, neither can possess the other's body. The target may not resist this spell's effect on him, though Absorption, Spell Resistance or similar magic might block it. Other magics such as Spirit Block or Spirit Screen (Spirit Shield) work normally to protect their users. Unlike spirit combat, the caster can break off or resume this attack at any time during the spell's duration.

blocks.name: Attain Perfection Of
blocks.text: All of the Attain Perfection spells are now Mysticism Talents (see Mysticism chapter, page XX).

blocks.name: Attract Attention
blocks.stat_line: Area (Tens of Metres), Cost 3, Duration (Rounds), Rank Priest, Resist (Willpower), Runes [three rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: Attract Attention forces everyone within the area of effect to resist the spell or direct their attention towards the caster. Affected enemies are forced to redirect attacks towards the caster, whilst neutral or friendly victims merely pay rapt attention to whatever the caster does. If out of reach of their new target, enemies will attempt to close the distance or use some other form of ranged attack or magic instead - however this does not prevent them from defending themselves if attacked by a third party. Members of the same cult as the caster are immune to the spell's effect.

blocks.name: Aurora
blocks.stat_line: Area (Metres), Cost 2, Duration (Hours), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell can only be cast whilst the Dawn Star is visible in the sky. Within the area shimmers pale, ephemeral sheets of light which infuse those inside its boundaries with a sense of hope and confidence. Those standing under the aurora are granted protection against the forces of Darkness and Chaos, reducing the Intensity of magic associated with either Rune, by half the Intensity of the spell. The spell ends when the sun rises, no matter how long the Aurora spell has left to go.

blocks.name: Axe Trance
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Rounds), Ranged (Touch), Rank Initiate, Runes [Death glyph, second rune glyph visible]
blocks.text: Akin to Arrow Trance, this spell turns the caster into a near unstoppable warrior, provided they are armed with an axe. Any attack roll has its Level of Success boosted by one step. Thus a Fumble upgrades to a Fail, a Fail to a Success and a Success to a Critical, whilst rolled Critical Successes cannot be increased any higher. Under its influence, the caster is unable to utilise any other weapon, save for their Unarmed skill (although a shield could used to passively

blocks.name: Axis Mundi
blocks.stat_line: Area (Metres), Cost 1, Duration (Hours), Rank Initiate, Runes [ancestor/man-like glyph visible]
blocks.text: Axis Mundi creates a holy sanctuary which enables free passage of ancestral spirits to the mundane plane, without the benefit of a Visibility spell or application of the shamanic Trance skill. In addition, the manifested ancestor can freely converse, cast magic and even engage in spirit combat whilst inside the sanctuary. It takes time for the desired ancestors to show up, however. Games Masters should roll 5d6 to determine how many minutes of singing, dancing or other ritual invocation is required before they manifest.

blocks.name: Bat Wings
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Minutes), Rank Initiate, Runes [Darkness glyph, Moon glyph]
blocks.text: This spell causes a pair of large bat wings to sprout from the back of the caster, enabling them to fly at a Movement Rate of 12. The wings have an inherent Armour Point value equal to half the Intensity of the spell, Hit Points equal to the caster's arms, and if damaged they use the Winged Biped Hit Location table (RuneQuest page 443). Bat wings are tinted a particular colour relevant to the providing cult.

blocks.name: Bear Fruit
blocks.stat_line: Area (Metres), Cost 1, Duration (Instant), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: Use the Ripen miracle (RuneQuest page 275).

blocks.name: Beastmaster
blocks.stat_line: Cost 2, Duration (Hours), Ranged (Tens of Metres), Rank Initiate, Resist (Endurance), Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell may be cast upon one or more non-intelligent animals. If the targets fail to resist then they will do whatever the caster directs for the duration, though they will refuse to obey obviously suicidal commands, and neither can they be commanded to fight on his behalf (but might do so instinctively). On the other hand, the beasts will not attack the caster. Beastmaster affects a number of creatures equal to the Intensity of the spell.

blocks.name: Become (Specific Other Shape)
blocks.stat_line: Cost 3, Duration (Hours), Rank Priest, Resist (Endurance), Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell comes in many forms. It turns the caster's shape into that specified by the spell. They retain all their old characteristics and skills, except for SIZ, which the caster chooses. The new SIZ cannot be larger than usual, but it can be smaller. In return the caster gains all the abilities (or lack of same) of the new shape. There are multiple versions of this spell, each permitting only one type of shape, anything from an animal, tree, rock, a dirty shirt or even a pile of dung!

blocks.name: Bee's Head
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Minutes), Ranged (Touch), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell turns the recipient's head into that of a gigantic bee. This allows them the ability to gain nourishment feeding from flowers. In addition, fluids (including beer, honey, water, etc.) can be drunk by the recipient and stored internally without harming the liquid, up to a total volume equal to the Intensity of the spell in litres. The liquid can be stored safely past the spell's duration, but must be regurgitated before the user can ingest any other substances.

blocks.name: Beetle's Head
blocks.stat_line: Cost 1, Duration (Minutes), Ranged (Touch), Rank Initiate, Runes [two rune glyphs visible]
blocks.text: This spell turns the recipient's head into that of a giant beetle, with spiked mandibles. He gains natural armour on the head equal to half the Intensity of the spell. He also gains a Bite attack as per a karg beetle (page 182), Size L, Reach M, doing 1d8 damage plus his Damage Modifier. This bite attack can be used in addition to normal weapon attacks and parries, provided the opponent is within reach.

blocks.name: Beetle's Limbs
blocks.text: See Sprout Arms (page 113), with the addition that the new limbs possess natural Armour Points equal to half the Intensity of the spell.

blocks.name: Berserk/Berserker
blocks.stat_line: Cost 2, Rank Initiate, Runes [Death glyph, Disorder glyph]
blocks.text: Use the default Berserk miracle (RuneQuest page 266). Note that worshipers of pacifistic cults such as Chalana Arroy
